text.title: Tuckman Team Development Stages
text.summary: Five Tuckman stages with PM-action menus, diagnostic evidence patterns, and a stage-transition trigger rule.

rule.statement: Re-run the Tuckman stage assessment after every team-composition change (hire, departure, reorg) — not on a calendar schedule.
rule.rationale: Teams regress to Forming or Storming when key members change; calendar-based assessment misses the regression window where intervention has the highest return.

section.title: Forming
section.p: Polite, cautious, high dependency on the PM for direction. Members focus on defining their role rather than executing work.

list.item: PM actions: provide explicit direction, clarify roles and responsibilities, set working agreements in writing
Evidence signals: members ask permission for small decisions, few spontaneous collaborations, low conflict but also low velocity

section.title: Storming
section.p: Conflict, competition, and frustration surface. This is the highest-risk stage — teams can stay here for months without structured facilitation.

list.item: PM actions: facilitate structured discussion, mediate (meet separately first), focus conversation on shared goals not positions, document agreed decisions
Evidence signals: heated retros, PR review comments with sharp tone, recurring same argument, velocity drop, attendance issues

rule.statement: Document Storming-stage decisions in writing immediately; unrecorded verbal resolutions get re-litigated within two sprints.
rule.rationale: Heated decisions feel resolved in the moment but lack shared memory; written record prevents the "we never agreed to that" pattern.

section.title: Norming
section.p: Trust building, cooperation, shared processes forming. Team starts solving problems without PM involvement.

list.item: PM actions: delegate, support, encourage cross-team ownership, step back from day-to-day mediation
Evidence signals: team resolves issues in standup without escalation, PR reviews are constructive, velocity stabilizes

section.title: Performing
section.p: High productivity, autonomy, self-organizing. PM focuses on removing systemic obstacles, not directing work.

list.item: PM actions: empower decisions, remove organizational blockers, protect from external interruptions, provide strategic context
Evidence signals: team ships without PM in daily loop, retros produce non-trivial improvements, velocity at or above target

section.title: Adjourning
section.p: Project closure or team restructuring. Focus on knowledge capture and individual transition.

list.item: PM actions: celebrate accomplishments, facilitate knowledge transfer, document lessons learned, support individual transitions
Evidence signals: project marked complete or team restructuring announced

antipattern.description: Using Tuckman stage labels as an excuse for inaction ("we're just Storming, it will pass").
antipattern.reason: Storming does not self-resolve without facilitation; teams that skip active mediation can stay in Storming for months and lose key contributors.

antipattern.description: Applying Tuckman to distributed async-only teams without adjustment.
antipattern.reason: The model assumes synchronous dynamics; async teams show attenuated stage signals and require additional indicators (response lag, async thread sentiment).
